ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for lamb rib-loin cutlet raw; the broader lamb meat food product term (FOODON_03311349) is used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LN covers sheep meat broadly; no specific rib-loin cutlet subdivision exists in the publicly available FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for raw lamb rib-loin cutlet without risk of code fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single raw lamb rib-loin cutlet with bone weighs approximately 120 g, providing around 280 kcal, 22 g protein, and 21 g fat before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Lamb, ribloin cutlet, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database (Record F06050018)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F06050018 at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat (7.9 g), monounsaturated fat (7.3 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.2 g), and trans fat (0.1 g) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17082 (Lamb, domestic, rib, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, raw) as the closest approved secondary source, as the HPB record does not report fatty acid fractions.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed per HPB Singapore Foodo F06050018 and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17082
gi_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; USDA FoodData Central
gi_source.source_match_notes: Lamb rib-loin cutlet, raw contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by HPB Singapore Foodo (F06050018) and consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17082. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value exists or is needed; glycemic load per serving is 0.0. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list a GI value for pure meat products with zero carbohydrate content, which is consistent with the not-applicable classification.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (54 mg/100 g) is favourable, but saturated fat (7.9 g/100 g) contributes to arterial stiffness and endothelial dysfunction, raising cardiovascular risk relevant to hypertension management.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension may include raw lamb rib-loin cutlet in moderation (≤100 g cooked portion), provided all visible fat is trimmed before cooking, no salt is added during preparation, and high-sodium sauces are avoided.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content produce no glycaemic impact; high protein (18.6 g/100 g) supports satiety and glycaemic stability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Lamb rib-loin cutlet is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes as a protein source with no glycaemic load; fat trimming is recommended to limit caloric density and saturated fat intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 7.9 g/100 g and cholesterol at 66 mg/100 g elevate LDL cholesterol risk; trans fat at 0.1 g/100 g further contributes to atherogenic risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of lamb rib-loin cutlet to occasional small portions (≤85 g), trim all visible fat before cooking, and prioritise lean protein alternatives such as skinless poultry or oily fish rich in omega-3 fatty acids.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat (7.9 g/100 g) exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; lamb is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) that drives pro-inflammatory cytokine production; trace trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) further worsens the inflammatory profile.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit lamb rib-loin cutlet; if consumed, trim all visible fat, use anti-inflammatory marinades (turmeric, ginger, garlic), and substitute with lean poultry, fatty fish, or plant proteins where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamb rib-loin cutlet, raw, is a bone-in cut of lamb taken from the rib and loin section of the carcass. It is sold raw and uncooked, displaying a deep red muscle colour with visible marbling and both internal and external fat deposits. The cut is prized for its tenderness and rich flavour, which intensifies on grilling, pan-frying, or roasting. Per 100 g, it provides 233 kcal, 18.6 g protein, 17.8 g total fat (of which approximately 7.9 g is saturated fat and 0.1 g is trans fat), and 0 g carbohydrate, making it a zero-glycaemic, high-protein food. Sodium is low at 54 mg/100 g, and potassium is 291 mg/100 g, yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio. Cholesterol is 66 mg/100 g. The cut is classified as halal and is commonly used in Mamak-style cooking in Singapore and Malaysia, as well as in Middle Eastern, South Asian, and Western cuisines. Clinically, the high saturated fat content is a concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis management, while the zero carbohydrate profile makes it suitable for type 2 diabetes and ketogenic dietary patterns. Fat trimming before cooking significantly reduces the saturated fat load.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a raw lamb cutlet?
Is lamb cutlet suitable for diabetics?
Is lamb cutlet high in saturated fat?
Can I eat lamb cutlet on a keto diet?
Is lamb cutlet good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F06050018) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17082, lamb domestic rib, lean and fat, raw) as the closest approved secondary source. All values are consistent between sources.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as lamb is a pure animal protein and fat food with no carbohydrate content; confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo (F06050018) and consistent with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17082). Sugar_g is naturally zero for the same reason. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as this is an animal muscle meat product with no plant-derived fibre. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g); gi_category set to not_applicable. Saturated_fat_g (7.9 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (7.3 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2 g), and trans_fat_g (0.1 g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17082 (lamb, domestic, rib, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/4 inch fat, raw) as the closest approved secondary source, as the HPB record does not report fatty acid fractions. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin, mineral, and moisture data not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo record for this specific cut; these fields are conservatively set to -1 pending a direct exact-match source for this specific HPB-catalogued cut. Primary cuisine tag is mamak as per input record; this is a raw ingredient used across multiple cuisines and the mamak tag reflects its common use context in Singapore. G6 multilingual compliance: this food is a generic raw ingredient (not a Singaporean dish), so mandatory multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not required under the G6 cuisine-based rule.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English. This is a generic raw ingredient, not a Singaporean dish, so no mandatory multilingual local names are required under G6. Aliases are comprehensive.

Primary source is HPB Singapore Foodo F06050018 (exact match, approved source). Secondary source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 17082 (approved source) for fatty acid fractions. Zero carbohydrate justified in notes as naturally zero.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0), sodium_mg (54.0), saturated_fat_g (7.9), sugar_g (0.0) all present as numeric values. Values are plausible and supported by HPB and USDA SR Legacy.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index set to null (not 0) per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0. gi_source documents the not-applicable rationale with approved source references (HPB and USDA). source_match_type set to analogous_estimate which is an allowed value per the template.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03311349 (lamb meat food product) used as broader anchor with gap note and match_type closest_parent. FoodEx2 A00LN (meat from sheep) used as broader anchor with gap note and match_type closest_parent. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters, covers what the food is, preparation methods, taste/texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate), Type 2 Diabetes (low), Cardiovascular Disease (high). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance. human_condition_name for type2_diabetes is 'Type 2 Diabetes'. Additional rheumatoid arthritis condition preserved from input.

All strings in aliases_by_language.english appear verbatim in the flat aliases array. No mandatory multilingual aliases required as this is not a Singaporean dish.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'broader' to 'closest_parent'.

Nutrient source provenance is fully traceable to approved primary (HPB F06050018) and secondary (USDA SR Legacy NDB 17082) sources. All other criteria remain valid from iteration 2.
